text.content: State of the art and practice in AI in education

title.plain_text: State of the art and practice in AI in education

blocks.text: Abstract

text.content: Abstract

rich_text.plain_text: Abstract

blocks.text: There are problems with the current understanding of AI in education. This may be due to lack of technical knowledge or just a strict look at what education is. Authors develop a typology of Ai in education and learning (AIED) to describe the 2022 state of the art of how AI is used in education.

text.content: There are problems with the current understanding of AI in education. This may be due to lack of technical knowledge or just a strict look at what education is. Authors develop a typology of Ai in education and learning (AIED) to describe the 2022 state of the art of how AI is used in education.

blocks.text: Introduction

text.content: Introduction

rich_text.plain_text: Introduction

blocks.text: The proeminence of artificial intelligence (AI) is ever increasing but there is plenty of misinformation surrounding it. There have been many advancements when it comes to the attention that AI has received over the years, not only from the perspective of policy and regulations, but specific applications as is the case with Ai in education - both in the perspective of using it for teaching or learning, but AI literacy in general. In the context of education, it has been argued that AI can not only automate teacher tasks, but augment human cognition as well.

text.content: The proeminence of artificial intelligence (AI) is ever increasing but there is plenty of misinformation surrounding it. In the context of education, it has been argued that AI can not only automate teacher tasks, but augment human cognition as well.

rich_text.plain_text: The proeminence of artificial intelligence (AI) is ever increasing but there is plenty of misinformation surrounding it. There have been many advancements when it comes to the attention that AI has received over the years, not only from the perspective of policy and regulations, but specific applications as is the case with Ai in education - both in the perspective of using it for teaching or learning, but AI literacy in general. In the context of education, it has been argued that AI can not only automate teacher tasks, but augment human cognition as well.

blocks.text: AI in education has been mostly pushed by more technical focused researchers but, more recently, has become more and more mainstream, and there is plenty of reason to be skeptical about its repercussions for practice. One such example is Intelligent tutoring systems (ITS - which are systems that are mainly focused on automated feedback and personalized learning experiences). AI systems in education operate under the assumption that learning comes from the development of problem solving capacity leveraging the efficiency of the human mind. Problem solving itself can be boiled down to finding a chain of heuristics that minimize the distance between expected result and known solutions - understand the problem, plan, execute the plan, look back and reflect on progress.

blocks.text: Historically, AIED focused on student comes from automated feedback systems on multiple choice questions - since there is an obvious right answer, setting the heuristics is relatively simple - allowing for immediate feedback to the student and, more importantly, opening the door to automate the correction of grading events. The evolution of ITS encompassed the development of other tools: Skinner (1950’s created a teaching machine that required students to build their own answers, thus acting more like a private tutor. Pask developed a system to train keyboard operators that gave them tasks based on previous performance. The first real explicit application of computer-aided instruction comes from a PhD thesis by Carbonell in 1970 who generated responses to student statements by looking at the semantic relationships between the response and a network of concepts that it was trained on.

blocks.text: Nowadays, AIED in the context of teaching is now spread throughout multiple different disciplines: collaborative learning or LA and EDM. Moreover, there is even potential for the use of AI for administrative or teacher support purposes, opening the door for new pedagogical approaches.

blocks.text: So, what are the motivations of the usage AI?

text.content: So, what are the motivations of the usage AI?

rich_text.plain_text: So, what are the motivations of the usage AI?

blocks.text: One motivation may be in promoting learning gains in specific knowledge domains independently of who is the human teacher. For example, it is possible to assess the student before he came into contact with a topic, teach it and assess the progress afterwards - which is especially useful when the knowledge that needs to be acquired is pre-defined. Common ITS focus on test preparation. Acquisition of pre-defined knowledge is, in fact, the most focused function of AIED, with socialization and individuation receiving less attention.

blocks.text: Bloom’s (1968) Mastery Learning is a key pedagogical model for student-focused AIED. Students start with different prior knowledge and capabilities, so they would need different amounts of instruction to achieve the same level of mastery. AIED systems like ITS are meant to provide this personalized component to bring the student to expected level of mastery. Using the mastery learning model, Bloom found that students receiving personalized feedback had almost 2 standard deviations of increased learning gains relative to whole-class teaching. As human instruction tends to result in below 1 standard deviation differences.

blocks.text: The effectiveness of ITS is a hotely debated topic in AIED research. Most work shows small to moderate learning gains in relatively small settings. Also, effectiveness  of ITS has also been tied to the prior knowledge of the student. Empyrical evidence from the US Department of Education shows that one the best known ITS (Cognitive Tutor) has translated, at the group level, in arguably mixed or even minor learning gains. However, there is a lot of recognized potential for AIED:

blocks.text: What is AI?

text.content: What is AI?

rich_text.plain_text: What is AI?

blocks.text: From an academic perspective, AI has no universally accepted definition and definitions tend to be context dependent. The political understanding of AI focuses on products accessible to the masses. The UNICEF’s definition of AI refers to them as machine based systems that can make decisions, predictions or reccomendations that influence real or virual environments and show an ability to adapt their behaviour from context. A key aspect in this definition stems from the fact the objectives of AI are human defined. This definition, altough brief, encompasses the current knowledge of AI and AI based systems.

blocks.text: Data-driven AI

text.content: Data-driven AI

rich_text.plain_text: Data-driven AI

blocks.text: Data-driven AI looks at very large amounts of data and uses them for optimization of a specific criterion. Training becomes optimizing a set of parameters or functions for something. In data-driven AI the optization is done using Calculus - the goal being to find the set of parameters that would minimize some loss function. Data-driven AI systems tend to vary on their optimization function and the amount of parameters that we need to optimize.

blocks.text: In essence, data-driven AI has seen a lot of sucess due to external factors that have allowed it be successful. However, these AI systems seem to be very inefficient and, at the end of the day, do not seem to actually represent actual knowledge or reasoning.

blocks.text: Knowledge-based AI

text.content: Knowledge-based AI

rich_text.plain_text: Knowledge-based AI

blocks.text: However, there are other relevant fields of AI such as symbolic or knowledge-based AI. These systems are created in order to attempt to mirror what could be called “expert-decision making”. These systems tend to be less generalizable for multiple domains, especially in comparison with data-driven AI.

text.content: However, there are other relevant fields of AI such as symbolic or knowledge-based AI. These systems are created in order to attempt to mirror what could be called “expert-decision making”. These systems tend to be less generalizable for multiple domains, especially in comparison with data-driven AI.

rich_text.plain_text: However, there are other relevant fields of AI such as symbolic or knowledge-based AI. These systems are created in order to attempt to mirror what could be called “expert-decision making”. blocks.text: Knowledge-based systems update use an inference engine to create a model - these models are more heuristics-based, meaning that different algorithms use different criteria to make a decision. However, these models tend to mirror what is known about the knowledge-domain in question. Ultimately, these systems can be understood as a set of sequences of if-then rules.

blocks.text: ITS

text.content: ITS

rich_text.plain_text: ITS

blocks.text: Intelligent tutoring systems offer step-by-step tutorials in structured subjects as is the case mathematics that are “personalized” for each student. In short, as students engage with the system by clicking and participating in the contents, the system creates a personalized pathway that aims to address the difficulties and misconceptions revealed by the student.

The results on whether it helps students or it harms them is still unclear and there will, for sure, be plenty of research as this is a very hot topic in education.

blocks.text: Apps that use NLP to analyse long-form student answers and provide personalized feedback. Due to the natural difficulty associated with processing text, there still are few commercially available tools that allow for this. There are, currently, automated AFA autograders that analyze student answers in coding exercises. Surprisingly, feedback from the machine has been received more positively than the feedback provided by a human professor.

Surprisingly, feedback from the machine has been received more positively than the feedback provided by a human professor.

blocks.text: Systems that promote learning not by providing answers to the student’s questions but rather by asking new questions in order to allow them to get to the correct answer themselves. Thus, these systems simulate the type of interaction that would occur between a student and a human tutor - acting as facilitators instead of mere sages.

Thus, these systems simulate the type of interaction that would occur between a student and a human tutor - acting as facilitators instead of mere sages.

blocks.text: Exploratory Learning Environments

text.content: Exploratory Learning Environments

rich_text.plain_text: Exploratory Learning Environments

blocks.text: While ITS and DBTS offer a step-by-step approach, ELE encourage students to explore and manipulate different elements of the learning environment. Exploratory learning is controversial in the literature however, with some authors arguing that the lack of direction may lead to poor learning outcomes due to cognitive overload.

blocks.text: Classroom monitoring

text.content: Classroom monitoring

rich_text.plain_text: Classroom monitoring

blocks.text: Some applications have been developed in order to identify whether students are paying attention or not. For example, there is evidence of video recordings being used to monitor where students are looking at. Other applications include the use of electroencephalography headsets to record brain activity, thus monitoring attention. Another application uses the school’s app to monitor where students are and spend time while on campus.

blocks.text: Automatic Summative Assessment

text.content: Automatic Summative Assessment

rich_text.plain_text: Automatic Summative Assessment

blocks.text: Autograders are a very popular area of research and there are multiple options currently available. Usually their application has been found to achieve great results in written tasks in very specific fields such as computer science or mathematics. While the efficacy of autograders has seen great developments over the years, it is still controversial and there is and its use in high-stakes testing poses a significant high-risk use case.

blocks.text: AI Teaching Assistants

text.content: AI Teaching Assistants

rich_text.plain_text: AI Teaching Assistants

blocks.text: AI-powered tools that are meant to take over teaching tasks. This can be either via the use of LLMs or chatbots that take student questions, thus giving time to educators for other tasks even if, arguably, it limits the role of the professor. Professors can also use these tools to develop their own knowledge.

blocks.text: Admissions

text.content: Admissions

rich_text.plain_text: Admissions

blocks.text: AI system applications that are used to determine whether a student should be admitted at the institution or to a specific program. There was an attempt to use a system called GRADE at the university of Austin-Texas. However, the program was discontinued because it replicated the same issues it was meant to address (probably because the training data incorporated those biases).

blocks.text: Systems that use AI to ensure that academic integrity is mantained on remote learning environments. blocks.text: Ethics

text.content: Ethics

rich_text.plain_text: Ethics

blocks.text: There are close to 80 sets of ethical AI principles, usually centered around a human rights perspective. There are some works that focus on ethics in AI use in education, something that heavily contrasts with ethics in Learning Analytics. A council of Europe report notes some key relevant concerns:

text.content: There are close to 80 sets of ethical AI principles, usually centered around a human rights perspective. There are some works that focus on ethics in AI use in education, something that heavily contrasts with ethics in Learning Analytics. A council of Europe report notes some key relevant concerns:

rich_text.plain_text: There are close to 80 sets of ethical AI principles, usually centered around a human rights perspective. There are some works that focus on ethics in AI use in education, something that heavily contrasts with ethics in Learning Analytics. A council of Europe report notes some key relevant concerns:

blocks.text: Teaching, assessment and accreditation should not be delegated to an AI system,

text.content: Teaching, assessment and accreditation should not be delegated to an AI system,

rich_text.plain_text: Teaching, assessment and accreditation should not be delegated to an AI system,

blocks.text: Children should be able to opt out of being profiled and sent to a predetermined learning path,

rich_text.plain_text: Children should be able to opt out of being profiled and sent to a predetermined learning path,

blocks.text: Children should not be harmed in their education by not engaging with an AI system

blocks.text: The authors argue that AI research should not only focus on Data or data analysis techniques but also have a pedagogical perspective, thus arguing for human development as a starting point for an adequate ethical framework.

blocks.text: Personalization

text.content: Personalization

rich_text.plain_text: Personalization

blocks.text: While it has been discontinued for some time, the emergence of recommender systems has brought forward the implementation of personalized learning in education. Few AIED applications actually seem to make an effort to create actual custom pathways for students and, instead, seem to point to some homegeneization of the students.

blocks.text: Efficacy and Impact

text.content: Efficacy and Impact

rich_text.plain_text: Efficacy and Impact

blocks.text: Current evidence by some meta analysis claims that AI systems seem to overperform human teachers in large classes while, simultaneously, tend to underperform human tutors in one on one scenarios. However, most of these studies have been performed by the developers of the AI system on limited numbers of students.

text.content: Current evidence by some meta analysis claims that AI systems seem to overperform human teachers in large classes while, simultaneously, tend to underperform human tutors in one on one scenarios. However, most of these studies have been performed by the developers of the AI system on limited numbers of students.

rich_text.plain_text: Current evidence by some meta analysis claims that AI systems seem to overperform human teachers in large classes while, simultaneously, tend to underperform human tutors in one on one scenarios. However, most of these studies have been performed by the developers of the AI system on limited numbers of students.

blocks.text: Also, most works focus on practical results instead of answering fundamental questions such as “is AI needed in education”. Also, AIED research also needs to focus on measuring its impact to cognition and brain development.

blocks.text: Techno solutionism

text.content: Techno solutionism

rich_text.plain_text: Techno solutionism

blocks.text: It has been argued that AI can address issues with accessibility for those who do not have access to qualified teachers, especially in developing countries. However, many of these areas lack the most basic infrasctructure and resources that would even allow for that to take effect.

blocks.text: AIED colonialism

text.content: AIED colonialism

rich_text.plain_text: AIED colonialism

blocks.text: AI research is mostly conducted in the Global north using datasets built from people in the these developed countries, which exacerbates current biases and does not account for meaningful cultural differences outside of these areas. This may involve, but is not limited to: languages used, pedagogical practices. Ultimately, tools that are more advanced by more developed nations will end up crowding out local solutions.

This may involve, but is not limited to: languages used, pedagogical practices. Ultimately, tools that are more advanced by more developed nations will end up crowding out local solutions.
